subject: Physics
q: When an external resistance of $5\ \Omega$ is connected across terminals of a cell, a current of $0.25\text{ A}$ flows through it. When the $5\ \Omega$ resistor is replaced by a $2\ \Omega$ resistor, a current of $0.5\text{ A}$ flows through it. The internal resistance of the cell is _______ $\Omega$.
question: When an external resistance of $5\ \Omega$ is connected across terminals of a cell, a current of $0.25\text{ A}$ flows through it. When the $5\ \Omega$ resistor is replaced by a $2\ \Omega$ resistor, a current of $0.5\text{ A}$ flows through it. The internal resistance of the cell is _______ $\Omega$.
solution:
Let the EMF of the cell be $E$ and its internal resistance be $r$.

The current $I$ in the circuit is given by $I = \dfrac{E}{R + r}$.

For the first case, $R_1 = 5\ \Omega$ and $I_1 = 0.25\text{ A}$:

$0.25 = \dfrac{E}{5 + r} \Rightarrow E = 0.25(5 + r)$

For the second case, $R_2 = 2\ \Omega$ and $I_2 = 0.5\text{ A}$:

$0.5 = \dfrac{E}{2 + r} \Rightarrow E = 0.5(2 + r)$

Equating the expressions for $E$:

$0.25(5 + r) = 0.5(2 + r)$

$5 + r = 2(2 + r)$

$5 + r = 4 + 2r$

$r = 1\ \Omega$

Answer: $1$

q: Equivalent resistance between the adjacent corners of a regular <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>-sided polygon of uniform wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> would be :
question: Equivalent resistance between the adjacent corners of a regular <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>-sided polygon of uniform wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> would be :
solution:
Given the total resistance of the wire is R. If the polygon has n numbers of side, then the resistance r, as shown in the following figure, of each side can be written as

r= Rn.................(1)

Among all the resistances, n-1 resistances are connected in series combination, whereas, their equivalent resistance is connected in parallel with the nth resistance. The equivalent circuit diagram for the above set of resistances can be depicted as follows:

From the above equivalent circuit diagram, the net equivalent resistance Req for the entire combination can be calculated as follows-

1Req= 1n-1r+1r= 1n-1Rn+1Rn= nn-1R+nR= n2n-1RReq= n-1Rn2

subject: Physics
q: Three resistors of $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel and the combination is connected in series with a $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery of $1 \Omega$ internal resistance. The rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor is
question: Three resistors of $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel and the combination is connected in series with a $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery of $1 \Omega$ internal resistance. The rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor is
solution:
Resistors $4 \Omega, 6 \Omega$ and $12 \Omega$ are connected in parallel, its equivalent resistance $(R)$ is given by

$$
\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{12} \Rightarrow R=\frac{12}{6}=2 \Omega
$$
Again $R$ is connected to $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ battery whose internal resistance $r=1 \Omega$.
Equivalent resistance now,
$$
R^{\prime}=2 \Omega+1 \Omega=3 \Omega
$$
Current, $I_{\text {total }}=\frac{V}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1.5}{3}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~A}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& I_{\text {total }}=\frac{1}{2}=3 x+2 x+x=6 x \\
& \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Current through $4 \Omega$ resistor $=3 x$
$$
=3 \times \frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~A}
$$
Therefore, rate of Joule heating in the $4 \Omega$ resistor
$$
=I^2 R=\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 \times 4=\frac{1}{4}=0.25 \mathrm{~W}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ shows a full scale deflection for a current of 1 mA. The value of resistance required to convert this galvanometer into an ammeter, showing full scale deflection for a current of 5 mA, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
question: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ shows a full scale deflection for a current of 1 mA. The value of resistance required to convert this galvanometer into an ammeter, showing full scale deflection for a current of 5 mA, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
solution:
To convert a galvanometer to an ammeter, a shunt resistance $S$ is connected in parallel. For full-scale deflection:
Current through galvanometer: $I_g = 1$ mA
Total current: $I = 5$ mA
Current through shunt: $I_s = 5 - 1 = 4$ mA
Since voltage across both is equal: $I_g \cdot G = I_s \cdot S$
$0.001 \times 100 = 0.004 \times S$
$S = \frac{0.1}{0.004} = 25$ Ω

q: Mobility of electrons in a semiconductor is defined as the ratio of their drift velocity to the applied electric field. If, for an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>N</mtext></math>-type semiconductor, the density of electrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>19</mn></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and their mobility is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.6</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the resistivity of the semiconductor (since it is an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>N</mtext></math>-type semiconductor contribution of holes is ignored) is close to:
question: Mobility of electrons in a semiconductor is defined as the ratio of their drift velocity to the applied electric field. If, for an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>N</mtext></math>-type semiconductor, the density of electrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>19</mn></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and their mobility is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.6</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the resistivity of the semiconductor (since it is an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>N</mtext></math>-type semiconductor contribution of holes is ignored) is close to:
solution:
Resistivity ρ=EJ=Eneυd=1neμ

μ= mobility

ρ=11019×1.6×10-19×1.6=12.56 Ω m

=0.4 Ω m

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> galvanometer is shunted by a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The percentage of the total current which passes through the galvanometer is
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> galvanometer is shunted by a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The percentage of the total current which passes through the galvanometer is
solution:
Let G be resistance of galvanometer and I' be the current passing through the galvanometer produces full scale deflection. If I is the maximum current, and since, G and S are in parallel so, the potential difference between them will be same.

Then,

 I'G=I2S⇒72I'=8I2⇒I2=9I'⇒I=I'+I2=10I'

So the percentage of total current passes through galvanometer is 

I'I×100=I'10I'×100=10%

subject: Physics
q: An electrical bulb rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>, is connected in series with another bulb rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. If the voltage across combination is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, the power consumed by the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> bulb will be about _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>.
question: An electrical bulb rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>, is connected in series with another bulb rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>. If the voltage across combination is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, the power consumed by the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> bulb will be about _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math>.
solution:
Electric power is given by P=V2R, here, V is voltage and R is resistance.

Now, for each bulb, R1=V2P=2202100=484 Ω and  R2=V2P=220260=484106 Ω

Net current through the series combination is I=220484+484×106 A=0.171 A

Hence, the power consumed by the 100 W bulb is P1=I2R1=14.06 W

subject: Physics
q: The stored charge in the capacitor in steady state of the following circuit is ________ $\mu$C.
question: The stored charge in the capacitor in steady state of the following circuit is ________ $\mu$C.
solution:
In steady state, the capacitor acts as an open circuit, meaning no current flows through the branch containing the $100\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor. Consequently, no current flows through the $10\ \Omega$ resistor in series with it on the top right.

The voltage across the capacitor will be equal to the voltage across the $4\ \Omega$ vertical resistor.

Let's simplify the circuit to find this voltage. The $12\ \Omega$ resistor on the left is in parallel with the $12\ \text{V}$ battery, so the voltage across the rest of the circuit remains $12\ \text{V}$.

For the rightmost part of the active circuit, the $4\ \Omega$ top resistor, the $4\ \Omega$ vertical resistor, and the $2\ \Omega$ bottom resistor are in series. Their equivalent resistance is:
$R_{\text{right}} = 4\ \Omega + 4\ \Omega + 2\ \Omega = 10\ \Omega$

This $10\ \Omega$ equivalent resistance is in parallel with the $10\ \Omega$ vertical resistor in the middle. The equivalent resistance of this parallel combination is:
$R_{\text{mid}} = \dfrac{10 \times 10}{10 + 10} = 5\ \Omega$

Now, the total resistance of the main circuit connected to the $12\ \text{V}$ source is the sum of the $5\ \Omega$ top-left resistor, the $5\ \Omega$ equivalent middle resistance, and the $2\ \Omega$ bottom-left resistor:
$R_{\text{total}} = 5\ \Omega + 5\ \Omega + 2\ \Omega = 12\ \Omega$

The total current flowing from the $12\ \text{V}$ source into this main branch is:
$I = \dfrac{12\ \text{V}}{12\ \Omega} = 1\ \text{A}$

The voltage across the parallel combination (the middle section) is:
$V_{\text{mid}} = I \times R_{\text{mid}} = 1\ \text{A} \times 5\ \Omega = 5\ \text{V}$

This $5\ \text{V}$ is applied across the right branch which has a total resistance of $10\ \Omega$. The current through this branch is:
$I_{\text{right}} = \dfrac{5\ \text{V}}{10\ \Omega} = 0.5\ \text{A}$

The voltage across the $4\ \Omega$ vertical resistor is:
$V_{4\Omega} = I_{\text{right}} \times 4\ \Omega = 0.5\ \text{A} \times 4\ \Omega = 2\ \text{V}$

Since the capacitor is in parallel with this $4\ \Omega$ resistor (with no voltage drop across the $10\ \Omega$ resistor in its branch), the voltage across the capacitor is also $2\ \text{V}$.

The stored charge in the capacitor is:
$Q = C \times V = 100\ \mu\text{F} \times 2\ \text{V} = 200\ \mu\text{C}$

Answer: $200$

subject: Physics
q: A current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mA</mi></math> was passed through an unknown resistor which dissipated a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4.4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Dissipated power when an ideal power supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected across it is:
question: A current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mA</mi></math> was passed through an unknown resistor which dissipated a power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4.4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Dissipated power when an ideal power supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is connected across it is:
solution:
P=V2R=i2RResistance R=V2P=Pi2

Here,  Power P=4.4 W   and i=2 mA  

so R=4.44 ×10-6 Ω

When 11 V power supply is connected across R 

P′=1124.4×4×10-6

=11×10-5 W.

q: By what percentage will the illumination of the lamp decrease if the current drops by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>?
question: By what percentage will the illumination of the lamp decrease if the current drops by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>?
solution:
Power through the resistor is given by, P=I2R.

Now, Pint=I2intR

and Pfinal=0.8Iint2R

Therefore, % change in power =

Pfinal−PintPint×100=0.64−1×100=−36%

q: The value of unknown resistance $(x)$ for which the potential difference between $B$ and $D$ will be zero in the arrangement shown, is :
question: The value of unknown resistance $(x)$ for which the potential difference between $B$ and $D$ will be zero in the arrangement shown, is :
solution: In case of balanced Wheatstone Bridge $\begin{aligned} & \frac{V_{A B}}{V_{A D}}=\frac{V_{B C}}{V_{C D}} \Rightarrow \frac{12}{6+x}=\frac{0.5}{0.5} \\ & x=6 \Omega \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In a potentiometer arrangement, a cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> gives a balance point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire. This cell is now replaced by another cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The difference in balancing length of potentiometer wire in above conditions will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: In a potentiometer arrangement, a cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> gives a balance point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire. This cell is now replaced by another cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The difference in balancing length of potentiometer wire in above conditions will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
In a potentiometer, null point or balance point is defined as the point where the deflection of the galvanometer is zero. If emf of the unknown battery is ε then,

ε=(Potential Gradient)×(Balancing length l). Therefore,

ε1ε2=l1l2

⇒1.201.80=36l2

⇒l2=18×3=54 cm

Hence, difference in balancing length is l2-l1=54-36=18 cm

subject: Physics
q: Sliding contact of a potentiometer is in the middle of the potentiometer wire having resistance $R_p=1 \Omega$ as shown in the figure. An external resistance of $R_e=2 \Omega$ is connected via the sliding contact. The electric current in the circuit is :
question: Sliding contact of a potentiometer is in the middle of the potentiometer wire having resistance $R_p=1 \Omega$ as shown in the figure. An external resistance of $R_e=2 \Omega$ is connected via the sliding contact. The electric current in the circuit is :
solution:
The circuit can be considered as
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{R}_{\text {eq }}=0.5+\frac{0.5 \times 2}{2+0.5}=\left(\frac{5}{10}+\frac{10}{25}\right) \Omega \\ & =\frac{45}{50}=\frac{9}{10}=0.9 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{i}=\frac{0.9}{0.9}=1 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

question: A current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μA</mi></math> deflects the coil of a moving coil galvanometer through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. q: A battery of 6 V is connected to the circuit as shown below. The current I drawn from the battery is
question: A battery of 6 V is connected to the circuit as shown below. The current I drawn from the battery is
solution:

The equivalent resistance for the given circuit will be,

Req=3+3×6+63+3+6+6+2=6 Ω

Therefore, the current supplied by the battery is

q: The given potentiometer has its wire of resistance 10 Ω. When the sliding contact is in the middle of the potentiometer wire, the potential drop across 2 Ω resistor is:
question: The given potentiometer has its wire of resistance 10 Ω. When the sliding contact is in the middle of the potentiometer wire, the potential drop across 2 Ω resistor is:
solution:
20-V05+0-V05+20-V02=0

4+10=2 V05+V02

14=4 V0+5 V010

V0=1409Volt

Potential difference across 2 Ω resistor is 20-V0

That is 20-1409 V

Hence, answer is 409 Volt

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit 'a' is an arbitrary constant. The value of m for which the equivalent circuit resistance is minimum, will be x2. The value of x is _____ .
question: In the given circuit 'a' is an arbitrary constant. The value of m for which the equivalent circuit resistance is minimum, will be x2. The value of x is _____ .
solution:
Left part of the resistors are connected in parallel, hence R1=am3.

Similarly, for the right part of the resistors we can write, R2=a2m.

Therefore, equivalent resistance will be,

R=am3+a2m

Differentiating both sides w.r.t m to observe critical points, we get dRdm=a3+a-12m2=0

⇒a3=a2m2  ⇒m=32

Thus, x=3.

subject: Physics
q: A galvanometer having coil resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> shows a full scale deflection for a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. For it to measure a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the value of the shunt should be:
question: A galvanometer having coil resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> shows a full scale deflection for a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. For it to measure a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the value of the shunt should be:
solution:
Given G=10 Ω, Ig=3 mA and I=8 A.

Let's consider the following figure:

From the figure, we have

IgG=I-IgS⇒S=IgGI-Ig⇒S=3×10-3108-0.003=3.75×10-3 Ω

q: Two wires of same length and thickness having specific resistances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively are connected in parallel. question: Two wires of same length and thickness having specific resistances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively are connected in parallel. The effective resistivity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">ρ</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Ωcm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> to the nearest integer, is ___ .
solution:
∵ in parallel, Rnet=R1R2R1+R2

⇒ρl2A=ρ1lA×ρ2lAρ1lA+ρ2lA

⇒ρ2=6×36+3=2⇒ρ=4

q:
This question has Statement I and Statement II. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.

Statement - I : Higher the range, greater is the resistance of ammeter.
Statement - II : To increase the range of ammeter, additional shunt needs to be used across it.
question:
This question has Statement I and Statement II. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.

Statement - I : Higher the range, greater is the resistance of ammeter.
Statement - II : To increase the range of ammeter, additional shunt needs to be used across it.
options: Statement-<math><mrow><mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math><mrow><mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is false.
Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is false, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true.

Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is the correct explanation of Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math>.

Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is not the correct explanation of Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math>.
solution:
For higher range resistance should be less,so statement I is false.

To increase the range by n-times additional shunt should be placed S=Gn-1 where 'G' resistance of galvanometer

subject: Physics
q: The electric current through a wire varies with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mi>t</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The amount of electric charge crossed through a section of the wire in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
question: The electric current through a wire varies with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mi>t</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The amount of electric charge crossed through a section of the wire in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given that
Current I=I0+βt, I0=20 A and β=3 A s-1

The instantaneous current is given by

I=20+3t   ...1

Integrating equation (1), we have

dqdt=20+3t⇒∫0qdq=∫020(20+3t)dt⇒q=∫02020dt+∫0203tdt⇒q=20t+3t22020=1000 C

subject: Physics
q: Two resistors $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the gaps of bridge as shown in figure. The null point is obtained with the contact of jockey at some point on wire $X Y$. When an unknown resistor is connected in parallel with $3 \Omega$ resistor, the null point is shifted by 22.5 cm toward $Y$. The resistance of unknown resistor is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
question: Two resistors $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the gaps of bridge as shown in figure. The null point is obtained with the contact of jockey at some point on wire $X Y$. When an unknown resistor is connected in parallel with $3 \Omega$ resistor, the null point is shifted by 22.5 cm toward $Y$. The resistance of unknown resistor is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
solution:
In a Wheatstone bridge with 2Ω and 3Ω, the null point is found when $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{l}{L-l}$.
Initial balance: $\frac{2}{3} = \frac{l_1}{L-l_1}$ gives $l_1 = \frac{2L}{5}$.
For standard wire length $L = 100$ cm, $l_1 = 40$ cm.
When unknown resistance $R$ is connected in parallel with 3Ω, equivalent resistance becomes $\frac{3R}{3+R}$.
New null point: $\frac{2}{\frac{3R}{3+R}} = \frac{l_2}{L-l_2}$, which simplifies to $\frac{2(3+R)}{3R} = \frac{l_2}{L-l_2}$.
Null point shifts 22.5 cm toward Y: $l_2 = 40 + 22.5 = 62.5$ cm.
Substituting: $\frac{2(3+R)}{3R} = \frac{62.5}{37.5} = \frac{5}{3}$
$6(3+R) = 15R \Rightarrow 18 = 9R \Rightarrow R = 2$ Ω

subject: Physics
q: A battery with EMF $E$ and internal resistance $r$ is connected across a resistance $R$. The power consumption in $R$ will be maximum when :
question: A battery with EMF $E$ and internal resistance $r$ is connected across a resistance $R$. The power consumption in $R$ will be maximum when :
solution:
Power consumed in external resistance R is $P = \frac{E^2 R}{(R + r)^2}$.
To find maximum, differentiate with respect to R:
$\frac{dP}{dR} = E^2 \frac{(R + r)^2 - 2R(R + r)}{(R + r)^4} = E^2 \frac{r - R}{(R + r)^3}$.
$\frac{dP}{dR} = 0$ gives $R = r$.
This is the maximum power transfer theorem: maximum power is delivered when the external load resistance equals the source internal resistance.
